---
title: 'Lecture 7 Chemical Exposomics: Untargeted Metabolomics Data Analysis Workflow'
author: "Xiangping Lin"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

# =============================================================================
# Created and last updated by Xiangping Lin, 03/30/26
# Course: Introduction to Bioinformatics in Exposomics (03/16 - 04/02/2026) 
# Lecture 7 Chemical Exposomics: Untargeted Metabolomics Data Analysis Workflow
# AUTHOR: [Xiangping Lin, Stanford University] 
# For University of Oulu, Oulu, Finland
# DATE: 2026-03-30
# NOTICE: FOR TEACHING PURPOSES ONLY!
# This script is intended for classroom use. 
# Do not redistribute without permission.
# =============================================================================
#
# Dataset: MassCube-processed LC-MS data (reverse-phase negative ionization)
# Matrices: Plasma (n=14), Serum (n=15), Whole Blood (n=15)
# QC samples: Pooled QC (n=6), used for feature filtering
#
# Workflow overview:
#   1.  Load & inspect data
#   2.  Data cleaning & QC-based feature filtering
#   2b. Visualization: QC CV distribution & feature retention curve
#   2c. Feature annotation status (how many features have MS2 matches)
#   3.  Normalization: TIC vs PQN
#   4.  Centering & scaling: mean-centering, auto-scaling, Pareto scaling
#   5.  Visualization: normalization boxplot
#   6.  Visualization: PCA plots
# =============================================================================

# =============================================================================
# SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS
# =============================================================================
#
# ── R & RStudio ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#   R version:       >= 4.5.0  (download: https://cran.r-project.org)
#   RStudio version: >= 2022.07 (download: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop)
#
# ── R packages (installed automatically in the next section) ──────────────────
#   tidyverse  >= 2.0.0   (ggplot2, dplyr, tidyr, readr, purrr, stringr)
#   ggrepel    >= 0.9.0   (non-overlapping text labels)
#   ggprism    >= 1.0.4   (publication-style plot theme)
#   patchwork  >= 1.1.0   (multi-panel figure layout)
#
# ── Disk space ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#   Raw data zip:            ~11 GB  (ST002796_rawdata.zip)
#   Unzipped raw data:       ~22 GB  (estimated)
#   MassCube spectral DB:    ~3  GB  (MassCube_DB_2026-03-19.pkl)
#   aligned_feature_table:   ~50 MB  (aligned_feature_table.txt)
#   Total (pre-step):        ~50 GB  recommended free disk space
#
# ── Internet connection ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#   Required for the pre-step downloads. A stable broadband connection is
#   recommended given the large file sizes (~11 GB zip + ~3 GB database).
#
# ── Optional command-line tools (speeds up downloads and unzipping) ───────────
#   curl:  built-in on macOS and Windows 10+; available on all Linux distros
#   wget:  built-in on most Linux distros; install on macOS via Homebrew:
#            brew install wget
#   7-Zip: Windows only; download from https://www.7-zip.org
#            Required for fast unzipping on Windows (install to default path:
#            C:/Program Files/7-Zip/7z.exe)
#
# ── MassCube (pre-step only — not required for the R workflow) ────────────────
#   Python:   >= 3.8    (download: https://www.python.org or via Anaconda)
#   MassCube: >= 1.2.20  (install: pip install masscube)
#                        (GitHub:  https://github.com/huaxuyu/masscube)
#   Note: MassCube is only needed if you want to reprocess the raw .mzML files
#         from scratch (Steps 1–9 of the pre-step). The aligned_feature_table.txt
#         can be downloaded directly in Step 10, bypassing MassCube entirely.
# =============================================================================

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# PRE-STEP: Obtain the Raw Mass Spectrometry Data (Optional)
# =============================================================================
# The aligned_feature_table.txt used in this workflow was generated by
# processing raw LC-MS data with MassCube software. Students are encouraged
# to reproduce this step on their own. Run the code below to download the
# raw data and set up the required folder structure automatically.
#
# NOTE: The download is ~11 GB. Ensure you have sufficient disk space and a
# stable internet connection before running this section.
# =============================================================================

# ── Step 1: Set your working directory ────────────────────────────────────────
# Remove all objects in the global environment
rm(list = ls())
# Change this path to wherever you want the data to be saved.
data_dir <- "."   # current directory by default

# ── Download helper: fast_download() ──────────────────────────────────────────
# R's built-in download.file() is intercepted by RStudio's own downloader
# (.rs.downloadFile), which does not correctly handle the method= and extra=
# arguments — causing it to misuse the URL as the destfile path.
# To avoid this, we call curl and wget directly via system() with the full
# command as a single string, which bypasses RStudio's wrapper entirely.
# R's download.file() is used only as a last resort.
#
# Method priority:
#   1. curl  — macOS (built-in), Linux, Windows 10+
#              -L: follow redirects | -C -: resume | --progress-bar: progress
#   2. wget  — Linux / macOS (Homebrew)
#              -c: resume | --show-progress: progress
#   3. R's download.file() — fallback with 2-hour timeout
fast_download <- function(url, destfile, description = "file") {
  if (file.exists(destfile)) {
    message(description, " already exists, skipping: ", destfile)
    return(invisible(NULL))
  }

  message("Downloading ", description, "...")

  # Try curl — build full command as one string to avoid shell word-splitting
  curl_bin <- Sys.which("curl")
  if (nchar(curl_bin) > 0) {
    message("Using curl...")
    cmd <- paste(shQuote(curl_bin),
                 "-L -C - --progress-bar",
                 "-o", shQuote(destfile),
                 shQuote(url))
    ret <- system(cmd)
    if (ret == 0) { message("Done: ", destfile); return(invisible(NULL)) }
    message("curl failed (exit code ", ret, "), trying wget...")
  }

  # Try wget — same single-string approach
  wget_bin <- Sys.which("wget")
  if (nchar(wget_bin) > 0) {
    message("Using wget...")
    cmd <- paste(shQuote(wget_bin),
                 "-c --show-progress",
                 "-O", shQuote(destfile),
                 shQuote(url))
    ret <- system(cmd)
    if (ret == 0) { message("Done: ", destfile); return(invisible(NULL)) }
    message("wget failed (exit code ", ret, "), falling back to R...")
  }

  # Fallback: R's download.file() with extended timeout
  message("Using R download.file() — this may be slow for large files...")
  old_timeout <- getOption("timeout")
  options(timeout = 7200)
  on.exit(options(timeout = old_timeout), add = TRUE)
  tryCatch({
    download.file(url = url, destfile = destfile, mode = "wb")
    message("Done: ", destfile)
  }, error = function(e) {
    message("Download failed: ", conditionMessage(e))
    message("Please download manually from:\n  ", url,
            "\nand save to: ", destfile)
  })
}

# ── Step 2: Download raw data from Metabolomics Workbench (Study ST002796) ────
# read more: https://www.metabolomicsworkbench.org/data/DRCCMetadata.php?Mode=Project&ProjectID=PR001744
zip_url  <- "https://www.metabolomicsworkbench.org/studydownload/ST002796_rawdata.zip"
zip_file <- file.path(data_dir, "ST002796_rawdata.zip")

fast_download(zip_url, zip_file, description = "raw data zip (~11 GB)")

# ── Step 3: Unzip the downloaded archive ──────────────────────────────────────
# The zip file extracts directly to a C18neg\ folder with no parent wrapper.
# We extract into a named subdirectory ST002796_rawdata\ so the final layout is:
#   <data_dir>\ST002796_rawdata\C18neg\
#
# R's built-in unzip() is slow for large archives (~11 GB) because it processes
# files sequentially in pure R. We use the system's native unzip utility instead
# (unzip on macOS/Linux, 7-Zip on Windows), which is much faster. R's unzip()
# is used as a fallback if no system tool is found.
unzip_dir  <- file.path(data_dir, "ST002796_rawdata")
c18neg_dir <- file.path(unzip_dir, "C18neg")

fast_unzip <- function(zip_file, exdir) {
  dir.create(exdir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

  if (.Platform$OS.type == "windows") {
    # Windows: use 7-Zip if available (much faster than R's unzip)
    # Download 7-Zip from https://www.7-zip.org if not installed
    zip7 <- "C:/Program Files/7-Zip/7z.exe"
    if (file.exists(zip7)) {
      message("Using 7-Zip for fast extraction...")
      system2(zip7, args = c("x", shQuote(zip_file),
                             paste0("-o", shQuote(exdir)), "-y"))
      return(invisible(NULL))
    }
  } else {
    # macOS / Linux: use system unzip if available
    unzip_bin <- Sys.which("unzip")
    if (nchar(unzip_bin) > 0) {
      message("Using system unzip for fast extraction...")
      system2(unzip_bin, args = c("-q", shQuote(zip_file),
                                  "-d", shQuote(exdir)))
      return(invisible(NULL))
    }
  }

  # Fallback: R's built-in unzip (slower but always available)
  message("System unzip not found. Falling back to R's unzip() — this may be slow...")
  unzip(zip_file, exdir = exdir)
}

if (!dir.exists(unzip_dir)) {
  message("Unzipping archive into: ", unzip_dir)
  fast_unzip(zip_file, exdir = unzip_dir)
  message("Unzipped to: ", c18neg_dir)
} else {
  message("ST002796_rawdata folder already exists, skipping unzip: ", unzip_dir)
}

# ── Step 4: Create the MassCube project folder structure ──────────────────────
# MassCube expects the following layout:
#   <project_dir>/
#   └── data/        <-- all .mzML files go here (flat, no subfolders)
project_dir <- file.path(c18neg_dir, "nrplc_masscube")
mzml_dir    <- file.path(project_dir, "data")

dir.create(project_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create(mzml_dir,    recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
message("MassCube project folder created: ", project_dir)

# ── Step 5: Move sample .mzML files into the data folder ──────────────────────
# These are the MS1 survey scan files (one per biological sample and QC).
# They are located directly inside subfolders of C18neg\, but NOT inside
# the MS2\DDA subfolder (those are handled separately in Step 6).
move_mzml <- function(source_dir, dest_dir, label = "") {
  files <- list.files(
    path        = source_dir,
    pattern     = "\\.mzML$",
    recursive   = TRUE,
    full.names  = TRUE,
    ignore.case = TRUE
  )
  # Exclude files already inside the destination folder
  files <- files[!grepl(
    normalizePath(dest_dir, mustWork = FALSE),
    normalizePath(files,    mustWork = FALSE),
    fixed = TRUE
  )]
  if (length(files) == 0) {
    message(label, "No .mzML files found to move (may already be in destination).")
  } else {
    message(label, "Moving ", length(files), " .mzML file(s) to: ", dest_dir)
    for (f in files) {
      file.rename(from = f, to = file.path(dest_dir, basename(f)))
    }
    message(label, "Done.")
  }
}

# Source: all subfolders of C18neg\ EXCEPT MS2\DDA\ and nrplc_masscube\
sample_source_dir <- c18neg_dir
dda_dir           <- file.path(c18neg_dir, "MS2", "DDA")

# Temporarily exclude the DDA folder by listing only direct subfolders
# that are not MS2\ or nrplc_masscube\
sample_subdirs <- list.dirs(c18neg_dir, recursive = FALSE, full.names = TRUE)
sample_subdirs <- sample_subdirs[
  !basename(sample_subdirs) %in% c("MS2", "nrplc_masscube")
]

for (subdir in sample_subdirs) {
  move_mzml(subdir, mzml_dir, label = paste0("[", basename(subdir), "] "))
}

# ── Step 6: Move MS2/DDA .mzML files into the data folder ─────────────────────
# The MS2 data-dependent acquisition (DDA) files are stored separately in:
#   C18neg\MS2\DDA\
# These files contain the MS2 fragmentation spectra used by MassCube for
# spectral library matching and compound annotation.
if (dir.exists(dda_dir)) {
  move_mzml(dda_dir, mzml_dir, label = "[MS2/DDA] ")
} else {
  message("[MS2/DDA] Folder not found: ", dda_dir,
          "\n  Please check that the zip was fully extracted.")
}

# ── Step 7: Download the MassCube spectral library database ──────────────────
# MassCube requires a reference spectral library (.pkl file) for compound
# annotation. Download it from Zenodo and save it into the project folder.
# read more: https://zenodo.org/records/19121645
db_url  <- "https://zenodo.org/records/19121645/files/MassCube_DB_2026-03-19.pkl?download=1"
db_file <- file.path(project_dir, "MassCube_DB_2026-03-19.pkl")

fast_download(db_url, db_file, description = "MassCube spectral library database")

# ── Step 8: Download the MassCube parameters file ────────────────────────────
# The parameters.csv file defines all processing settings used by MassCube
# read more: https://huaxuyu.github.io/masscubedocs/docs/untargeted_metabolomics/parameters/
# Using this file ensures your results match the aligned_feature_table.txt
# provided with this workflow.
params_url  <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/victorlin2/teaching/main/university%20of%20oulu/Introduction%20to%20Bioinformatics%20in%20Exposomics%202026/parameters.csv"
params_file <- file.path(project_dir, "parameters.csv")

fast_download(params_url, params_file, description = "MassCube parameters file")

# ── Step 9: Download the MassCube sample table file ───────────────────────────
# The sample_table.csv tells MassCube which .mzML files correspond to which
# sample groups (e.g., Plasma, Serum, Whole Blood, QC). MassCube uses this
# to correctly label samples in the aligned feature table output.
# read more: https://huaxuyu.github.io/masscubedocs/docs/untargeted_metabolomics/generate_sample_table/
sample_table_url  <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/victorlin2/teaching/main/university%20of%20oulu/Introduction%20to%20Bioinformatics%20in%20Exposomics%202026/sample_table.csv"
sample_table_file <- file.path(project_dir, "sample_table.csv")

fast_download(sample_table_url, sample_table_file, description = "MassCube sample table")

# ── Step 10: Run MassCube (manual step — cannot be automated from R) ──────────
# MassCube is a standalone Python-based software.
# R cannot launch it directly. Please follow these steps manually:
#
#   1. Install MassCube if not already installed:
#      pip install masscube   (in your Python/Anaconda environment)
#      or download from: https://github.com/huaxuyu/masscube
#
#   2. Open MassCube and set the project directory to:
#         <your data_dir>/ST002796_rawdata/C18neg/nrplc_masscube/
#
#   3. Run the full untargeted metabolomics pipeline.
#      MassCube will output aligned_feature_table.txt in the project folder.
#
# Alternatively, use the aligned_feature_table.txt provided with this workflow,
# which was generated using exactly these steps.

# ── Step 11: Download the pre-processed aligned feature table ────────────────
# If you prefer to skip raw data processing (Steps 1–9), download the
# aligned_feature_table.txt directly. This file is the MassCube output and
# is the starting point for the R workflow below.
aft_url  <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/victorlin2/teaching/main/university%20of%20oulu/Introduction%20to%20Bioinformatics%20in%20Exposomics%202026/aligned_feature_table.txt"
aft_file <- file.path(project_dir, "aligned_feature_table.txt")

fast_download(aft_url, aft_file, description = "aligned feature table")

message("Pre-step complete. Folder structure ready for MassCube processing.")
message("Project directory: ", project_dir)

# ── Install/load required packages ────────────────────────────────────────────
# install.packages(c("tidyverse", "ggrepel", "ggprism", "patchwork"))

library(tidyverse)   # data wrangling + ggplot2
library(ggrepel)     # non-overlapping text labels on plots
library(ggprism)     # clean publication-style ggplot theme
library(patchwork)   # combine multiple ggplots into panels

```

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 1: Load & Inspect Data
# =============================================================================
# The aligned_feature_table.txt is the output from MassCube software.
# Each row = one LC-MS feature (unique m/z + retention time combination).
# Columns = feature metadata + per-sample peak intensities.
#
# The file is located in the MassCube project folder created in the pre-step:
#   <data_dir>/ST002796_rawdata/C18neg/nrplc_masscube/aligned_feature_table.txt
#
# If you ran the pre-step, update data_dir below to match your Step 1 setting.
# If you are using a local copy, set the path directly.
data_dir    <- "."   # update to match your pre-step data_dir if needed
aft_path    <- file.path(data_dir, "ST002796_rawdata", "C18neg",
                         "nrplc_masscube", "aligned_feature_table.txt")

raw <- read.delim(aft_path,
                  sep = "\t", header = TRUE, check.names = FALSE,
                  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

cat("Raw dimensions:", nrow(raw), "features x", ncol(raw), "columns\n")

# ── Define metadata vs. sample columns ────────────────────────────────────────
# Metadata columns describe the chemical feature (m/z, RT, annotation, etc.)
meta_cols <- c("group_ID", "feature_ID", "m/z", "RT", "adduct",
               "isotope_state", "is_isotope", "is_in_source_fragment",
               "scan-scan-cor", "Gaussian_similarity", "noise_score",
               "asymmetry_factor", "peak_shape", "detection_rate",
               "detection_rate_gap_filled", "alignment_reference_file",
               "isotopes", "MS2_reference_file", "MS2_scan_id", "MS2",
               "precursor_ion_fraction", "matched_MS2", "search_mode",
               "annotation", "formula", "similarity", "matched_peak_number",
               "SMILES", "InChIKey")

# ── Classify sample columns ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# QC1-6: pooled QC samples injected throughout the run to monitor instrument
#        stability. Used for feature filtering (CV < 30%).
# QCDDA: data-dependent acquisition QC samples used only for MS2 identification.
#        These are EXCLUDED from quantitative analysis.
# PLASMA, SERUM, WB: biological samples from three different blood matrices.

qc_cols     <- c("QC1_C18neg", "QC2_C18neg", "QC3_C18neg",
                 "QC4_C18neg", "QC5_C18neg", "QC6_C18neg")

# Note: PLASMA04 is absent from this dataset
plasma_cols <- paste0("PLASMA", sprintf("%02d", c(1:3, 5:15)), "_C18neg")
serum_cols  <- paste0("SERUM",  sprintf("%02d", 1:15), "_C18neg")
wb_cols     <- paste0("WB",     sprintf("%02d", 1:15), "_C18neg")
bio_cols    <- c(plasma_cols, serum_cols, wb_cols)
all_cols    <- c(qc_cols, bio_cols)   # QC + biological (no QCDDA)

cat("QC samples:     ", length(qc_cols), "\n")
cat("Plasma samples: ", length(plasma_cols), "\n")
cat("Serum samples:  ", length(serum_cols), "\n")
cat("WB samples:     ", length(wb_cols), "\n")

# ── Extract feature metadata ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
feat_meta <- raw[, meta_cols]

```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 2: Data Cleaning & QC-Based Feature Filtering
# =============================================================================
# In untargeted metabolomics, the raw feature table contains many low-quality
# features that must be removed before analysis. We apply three sequential
# filters, then impute remaining missing values.

# ── Helper: convert matrix to numeric, replace 0 with NA ──────────────────────
# MassCube outputs 0 for undetected features; we treat these as missing (NA).
to_mat <- function(df, cols) {
  m <- as.matrix(df[, cols])
  m[m == 0] <- NA
  storage.mode(m) <- "numeric"
  m
}

mat_qc  <- to_mat(raw, qc_cols)
mat_bio <- to_mat(raw, bio_cols)
feat_meta_clean <- feat_meta

cat("\n=== Data Cleaning Attrition Table ===\n")
cat(sprintf("Starting features:                    %5d\n", nrow(raw)))

# ── Filter 1: Remove isotopes and in-source fragments ─────────────────────────
# MassCube flags isotope peaks (M+1, M+2, ...) and in-source fragments.
# These are not independent chemical features and should be removed.
#
# NOTE FOR EDUCATIONAL PURPOSES:
# For simplicity, we remove all features flagged by MassCube as isotope peaks
# or in-source fragments in a single step. In a real study, however, this
# decision is considerably more complex:
#
#   - Isotope detection (M+1, M+2, ...): Algorithms identify isotope peaks by
#     looking for features with the expected mass difference (~1.003 Da) and
#     correlated chromatographic profiles. This works well for abundant features
#     but can fail for low-intensity peaks or when two unrelated compounds
#     happen to co-elute with similar mass spacing. False positives (true
#     metabolites incorrectly flagged as isotopes) and false negatives (isotope
#     peaks that slip through) both occur with current tools.
#
#   - In-source fragment detection: In-source fragmentation produces ions that
#     look like independent features but are actually fragments of a larger
#     precursor generated in the ion source before MS1 detection. Detection
#     relies on co-elution and mass relationships, which are not always
#     definitive. Some genuine metabolites may share these characteristics.
#
# In practice, researchers often manually review flagged features, cross-check
# against MS2 spectra, and apply additional filters (e.g., minimum peak shape
# scores, adduct consistency checks) before making a final decision.
keep_primary <- raw$is_isotope == "False" & raw$is_in_source_fragment == "False"
mat_qc        <- mat_qc[keep_primary, ]
mat_bio       <- mat_bio[keep_primary, ]
feat_meta_clean <- feat_meta_clean[keep_primary, ]
cat(sprintf("After removing isotopes/in-source:    %5d  (-%d)\n",
            sum(keep_primary), nrow(raw) - sum(keep_primary)))

# ── Filter 2: Detection rate filter (>= 50% in at least one group) ────────────
# A feature must be detected in at least 50% of samples in at least one
# biological group. This removes noise-level features.
detect_rate <- function(mat) rowMeans(!is.na(mat))

dr_plasma <- detect_rate(mat_bio[, plasma_cols])
dr_serum  <- detect_rate(mat_bio[, serum_cols])
dr_wb     <- detect_rate(mat_bio[, wb_cols])

keep_detect <- (dr_plasma >= 0.5) | (dr_serum >= 0.5) | (dr_wb >= 0.5)
mat_qc        <- mat_qc[keep_detect, ]
mat_bio       <- mat_bio[keep_detect, ]
feat_meta_clean <- feat_meta_clean[keep_detect, ]
n_after_detect <- sum(keep_detect)
cat(sprintf("After detection rate filter (>=50%%):  %5d  (-%d)\n",
            n_after_detect, sum(keep_primary) - n_after_detect))

# ── Filter 3: QC CV filter (< 30%) ────────────────────────────────────────────
# The coefficient of variation (CV = SD/mean) across QC samples measures
# analytical reproducibility. Features with CV >= 30% are too variable to
# be reliably quantified and are removed.
qc_cv <- apply(mat_qc, 1, function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  if (length(x) < 2) return(NA)
  sd(x) / mean(x) * 100
})

keep_cv <- !is.na(qc_cv) & qc_cv < 30
mat_qc        <- mat_qc[keep_cv, ]
mat_bio       <- mat_bio[keep_cv, ]
feat_meta_clean <- feat_meta_clean[keep_cv, ]
n_after_cv <- sum(keep_cv)
cat(sprintf("After QC CV filter (<30%%):            %5d  (-%d)\n",
            n_after_cv, n_after_detect - n_after_cv))


# =============================================================================
# SECTION 2b: QC CV Distribution & Feature Retention Curve
# =============================================================================
# Before moving on, it is instructive to visualise the full CV distribution
# and show students how the choice of CV threshold affects how many features
# are retained. This is one of the most important QC decisions in an
# untargeted metabolomics workflow.
#
# We use the CV values computed BEFORE the filter was applied (qc_cv), so
# the plot reflects the complete distribution students are filtering from.
#
# Two panels:
#   A. Histogram of QC CVs with colour-coded quality zones and threshold lines
#   B. Feature retention curve: % features retained vs. CV threshold (0-100%)

# ── Number of features with a computable CV (>= 2 QC detections) ──────────────
n_cv_computable <- sum(!is.na(qc_cv))
n_cv_na         <- sum(is.na(qc_cv))

cat(sprintf("\nQC CV summary (%d features with >= 2 QC detections):\n",
            n_cv_computable))
print(summary(qc_cv[!is.na(qc_cv)]))
cat(sprintf("Features excluded from CV (< 2 QC detections): %d\n", n_cv_na))

# Retention at common thresholds (teaching reference)
cat("\nFeature retention at key CV thresholds:\n")
for (thr in c(20, 30, 50)) {
  n <- sum(!is.na(qc_cv) & qc_cv < thr)
  cat(sprintf("  CV < %2d%%:  %5d / %5d features  (%4.1f%%)\n",
              thr, n, n_cv_computable, n / n_cv_computable * 100))
}

# ── Data for plots ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cv_df <- data.frame(cv = qc_cv[!is.na(qc_cv)])

# Retention curve across the full 0-100% range
cv_seq    <- seq(1, 100, by = 0.5)
retention <- data.frame(
  threshold    = cv_seq,
  n_retained   = sapply(cv_seq, function(t) sum(cv_df$cv < t)),
  pct_retained = sapply(cv_seq, function(t) sum(cv_df$cv < t) / n_cv_computable * 100)
)

# Annotated key points on the retention curve
key_pts <- retention[retention$threshold %in% c(20, 30, 50), ]
key_pts$label <- paste0("CV<", key_pts$threshold, "%\n",
                         key_pts$n_retained, " features\n(",
                         round(key_pts$pct_retained, 1), "%)")

# ── Quality zone shading (shared between both panels) ─────────────────────────
# Zones reflect community conventions for LC-MS metabolomics:
#   Excellent < 20%  |  Good 20-30%  |  Acceptable 30-50%  |  Poor > 50%
shade_df <- data.frame(
  xmin  = c(0,  20, 30, 50),
  xmax  = c(20, 30, 50, 100),
  fill  = c("#75A025", "#E9ED4C", "#FF9400", "#FD9BED")
)

zone_labels <- data.frame(
  x     = c(10, 25, 40, 75),
  label = c("Excellent\n(<20%)", "Good\n(20-30%)",
            "Acceptable\n(30-50%)", "Poor\n(>50%)"),
  col   = c("#3a6e00", "#8a7a00", "#b35900", "#9b0070")
)

# ── Panel A: CV histogram ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
thresholds <- data.frame(
  thr = c(20, 30, 50),
  col = c("#8a7a00", "#b35900", "#9b0070"),
  lbl = c("CV = 20%", "CV = 30%", "CV = 50%")
)

p_hist <- ggplot(cv_df[cv_df$cv <= 100, , drop = FALSE], aes(x = cv)) +
  geom_rect(data = shade_df,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = fill),
            alpha = 0.18, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, colour = "white", fill = "#0279EE",
                 alpha = 0.75, linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_vline(data = thresholds,
             aes(xintercept = thr, colour = col),
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_identity() +
  # Zone labels at top of panel
  annotate("text", x = zone_labels$x, y = Inf, vjust = 1.4,
           label = zone_labels$label, size = 2.8,
           colour = zone_labels$col, fontface = "bold") +
  # Threshold labels on x-axis
  geom_text(data = thresholds,
            aes(x = thr, y = 0, label = lbl, colour = col),
            vjust = -0.4, hjust = -0.05, size = 2.8, fontface = "bold",
            inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), clip = "off") +
  labs(
    title    = "A  QC CV Distribution",
    subtitle = paste0("n = ", n_cv_computable,
                      " features with >= 2 QC detections | ",
                      n_cv_na, " features excluded (< 2 QC detections)"),
    x = "Coefficient of Variation across QC samples (%)",
    y = "Number of features"
  ) +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(plot.title    = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 8.5, colour = "grey40"),
        legend.position = "none")

# ── Panel B: Feature retention curve ──────────────────────────────────────────
p_ret <- ggplot(retention, aes(x = threshold, y = pct_retained)) +
  geom_rect(data = shade_df,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = fill),
            alpha = 0.18, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  geom_line(colour = "#0279EE", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_vline(data = thresholds,
             aes(xintercept = thr, colour = col),
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_identity() +
  geom_point(data = key_pts, aes(x = threshold, y = pct_retained),
             colour = c("#8a7a00", "#b35900", "#9b0070"),
             size = 3.5, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_label(data = key_pts,
             aes(x = threshold, y = pct_retained, label = label),
             hjust        = c(1.08, 1.08, -0.08), vjust = 0.5,
             size         = 2.6, label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines"),
             linewidth    = 0.3, fill = "white", colour = "grey20",
             inherit.aes  = FALSE) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 100, 10)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 100, 20),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title    = "B  Feature Retention vs. CV Threshold",
    subtitle = "Stricter threshold = fewer but more reproducible features",
    x = "CV threshold (%)",
    y = "Features retained (%)"
  ) +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(plot.title    = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 8.5, colour = "grey40"),
        legend.position = "none")

# ── Combine and save ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig_cv <- p_hist + p_ret +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "QC-Based Feature Filtering: CV Distribution and Retention",
    subtitle = paste0("Pooled QC samples (n=", length(qc_cols),
                      ") | Features after detection rate filter (n=",
                      n_after_detect, ")"),
    theme    = theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40")
    )
  )

ggsave("fig0_qc_cv_distribution.png", fig_cv,
       width = 14, height = 6, dpi = 180, bg = "white")
cat("Figure 0 saved: fig0_qc_cv_distribution.png\n")


# =============================================================================
# SECTION 2c: Feature Annotation Status
# =============================================================================
# MassCube performs spectral library matching against reference MS2 databases.
# Two search modes are used:
#
#   Identity search: exact match to a reference spectrum (high confidence).
#   Fuzzy search:    approximate match allowing for minor spectral differences
#                    (e.g., adduct variants, in-source losses). Lower confidence
#                    but greatly increases coverage.
#
# Only features with a spectral similarity score >= 0.70 are reported.
# The key message: the vast majority of features (~96%) remain unannotated.
# This is normal in untargeted metabolomics — the chemical space of human
# blood, especially in an exposomics context, far exceeds what is currently
# catalogued in reference libraries.

# ── Build annotation status summary ───────────────────────────────────────────
ann_df <- feat_meta_clean %>%
  mutate(
    ann     = trimws(annotation),
    has_ann = nchar(ann) > 0,
    mode    = trimws(search_mode),
    ann_status = case_when(
      !has_ann                  ~ "Unannotated",
      mode == "identity_search" ~ "MS2 identity match",
      mode == "fuzzy_search"    ~ "MS2 fuzzy match",
      TRUE                      ~ "Unannotated"
    )
  )

cat("\n=== Annotation status (post-QC features) ===\n")
print(table(ann_df$ann_status))

# ── Donut chart: annotation status ────────────────────────────────────────────
# The two annotated segments (1.1% and 3.1%) are too small to label directly
# on the donut without overlap. Instead, counts and percentages are embedded
# in the legend labels, which are always readable regardless of segment size.
status_df <- ann_df %>%
  count(ann_status, name = "n") %>%
  mutate(
    pct       = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1),
    ann_status = factor(ann_status,
      levels = c("MS2 identity match", "MS2 fuzzy match", "Unannotated"))
  ) %>%
  arrange(ann_status)

fig_ann <- ggplot(status_df, aes(x = 2, y = n, fill = ann_status)) +
  geom_col(width = 1, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  coord_polar(theta = "y", start = 0) +
  xlim(0.5, 2.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("MS2 identity match" = "#0279EE",
               "MS2 fuzzy match"    = "#FF9400",
               "Unannotated"        = "grey82"),
    name   = NULL,
    labels = c(
      "MS2 identity match" = "MS2 identity match  (n=143, 1.1%)",
      "MS2 fuzzy match"    = "MS2 fuzzy match  (n=423, 3.1%)",
      "Unannotated"        = "Unannotated  (n=12,966, 95.8%)"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  # Label only the large segment inside the donut
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = nrow(ann_df) * 0.52,
           label = "95.8%", size = 7, fontface = "bold", colour = "grey35") +
  # Center: total feature count
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0,
           label = "13,532\nfeatures",
           size = 5, fontface = "bold", colour = "grey25") +
  labs(
    title    = "Feature Annotation Status",
    subtitle = paste0("13,532 features after QC filtering  |  ",
                      "Only 4.2% have MS2 spectral matches\n",
                      "Most features in untargeted metabolomics remain unknown")
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5,
                                 margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, colour = "grey40", hjust = 0.5,
                                 margin = margin(b = 10)),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.text      = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.key.size  = unit(0.55, "cm"),
    legend.spacing.x = unit(0.3, "cm"),
    plot.margin      = margin(10, 20, 10, 20)
  )

ggsave("fig_annotation_summary.png", fig_ann,
       width = 9, height = 7, dpi = 180, bg = "white")
cat("Figure saved: fig_annotation_summary.png\n")


# ── Missing value imputation: half-minimum per feature ────────────────────────
# Remaining missing values are imputed with half the minimum observed value
# for that feature. This is a common approach in metabolomics that avoids
# introducing zeros while reflecting the detection limit.
impute_half_min <- function(mat) {
  t(apply(mat, 1, function(x) {
    half_min <- min(x, na.rm = TRUE) / 2
    x[is.na(x)] <- half_min
    x
  }))
}

mat_qc_imp  <- impute_half_min(mat_qc)
mat_bio_imp <- impute_half_min(mat_bio)
mat_all_imp <- cbind(mat_qc_imp, mat_bio_imp)

colnames(mat_qc_imp)  <- qc_cols
colnames(mat_bio_imp) <- bio_cols
colnames(mat_all_imp) <- all_cols

cat(sprintf("After half-min imputation:            %5d  (no features removed)\n",
            n_after_cv))
cat(sprintf("\nFinal clean dataset: %d features x %d biological samples\n",
            nrow(mat_bio_imp), ncol(mat_bio_imp)))

```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 3: Normalization
# =============================================================================
# Normalization corrects for systematic differences in total sample amount
# loaded onto the LC-MS instrument. We compare two common approaches:
#
#   TIC (Total Ion Current): divide each sample by its total signal sum,
#       then rescale to the median total. Simple and intuitive.
#
#   PQN (Probabilistic Quotient Normalization): uses the QC samples as a
#       reference profile. For each sample, compute the ratio of each feature
#       to the reference; the median ratio is the normalization factor.
#       More robust to features that are truly different between groups.
#
# After normalization, we apply log2 transformation to reduce the dynamic
# range and make the data more normally distributed.

# ── TIC Normalization ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
tic_norm <- function(mat) {
  col_sums <- colSums(mat, na.rm = TRUE)
  med_sum  <- median(col_sums)
  sweep(mat, 2, col_sums / med_sum, FUN = "/")
}

mat_tic_all <- tic_norm(mat_all_imp)

# ── PQN Normalization ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pqn_norm <- function(mat, ref_cols) {
  # Reference profile = median intensity of each feature across QC samples
  ref_profile <- apply(mat[, ref_cols], 1, median)
  ref_profile[ref_profile == 0] <- NA
  # Quotients: each sample's intensities divided by the reference
  quotients <- sweep(mat, 1, ref_profile, FUN = "/")
  # Normalization factor = median quotient per sample
  norm_factors <- apply(quotients, 2, median, na.rm = TRUE)
  sweep(mat, 2, norm_factors, FUN = "/")
}

mat_pqn_all <- pqn_norm(mat_all_imp, qc_cols)

# ── Log2 transformation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# After half-min imputation all values are strictly positive, so we use log2()
# directly. log2(x+1) is only needed when zeros are present; using it here
# would introduce a negligible but scientifically misleading pseudocount.
mat_raw_log2 <- log2(mat_all_imp[, all_cols])
mat_tic_log2 <- log2(mat_tic_all[, all_cols])
mat_pqn_log2 <- log2(mat_pqn_all[, all_cols])

# Biological samples only (for downstream analysis)
mat_pqn_log2_bio <- mat_pqn_log2[, bio_cols]

cat("Normalization complete.\n")
cat("PQN normalization factors (first 6 samples):\n")
ref_profile  <- apply(mat_all_imp[, qc_cols], 1, median)
ref_profile[ref_profile == 0] <- NA
quotients    <- sweep(mat_all_imp, 1, ref_profile, FUN = "/")
norm_factors <- apply(quotients, 2, median, na.rm = TRUE)
print(round(norm_factors[1:6], 3))

```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 4: Centering & Scaling
# =============================================================================
# After normalization, we can further preprocess the data by centering and
# scaling features. This affects how PCA (and other multivariate methods)
# weight different features.
#
#   No scaling:       raw log2-normalized values; high-intensity features
#                     dominate the analysis.
#
#   Mean centering:   subtract the mean of each feature. Centers the data
#                     around zero but does not equalize variance.
#
#   Auto-scaling (UV): mean-center then divide by SD. Gives every feature
#                     equal weight regardless of intensity. Can amplify noise
#                     from low-abundance features.
#
#   Pareto scaling:   mean-center then divide by sqrt(SD). A compromise
#                     between no scaling and auto-scaling. Recommended for
#                     metabolomics data.

# All scaling functions operate on features x samples matrices
scale_none   <- function(mat) mat

scale_center <- function(mat) {
  sweep(mat, 1, rowMeans(mat), "-")
}

scale_auto   <- function(mat) {
  feat_sds <- apply(mat, 1, sd)
  feat_sds[feat_sds == 0] <- NA
  m <- sweep(mat, 1, rowMeans(mat), "-")
  sweep(m, 1, feat_sds, "/")
}

scale_pareto <- function(mat) {
  feat_sds <- apply(mat, 1, sd)
  feat_sds[feat_sds == 0] <- NA
  m <- sweep(mat, 1, rowMeans(mat), "-")
  sweep(m, 1, sqrt(feat_sds), "/")
}

# ── PCA helper ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
run_pca <- function(mat) {
  mat_clean <- mat[complete.cases(mat), ]
  # center=TRUE ensures PCA is always mean-centered (required for valid PCA).
  # For scaling variants that already subtract the mean, this is redundant
  # but harmless. For "No additional scaling", it is essential.
  pca <- prcomp(t(mat_clean), center = TRUE, scale. = FALSE)
  var_exp <- round(summary(pca)$importance[2, 1:2] * 100, 1)
  list(scores = as.data.frame(pca$x[, 1:2]), var_exp = var_exp)
}

# ── Sample metadata for plotting ──────────────────────────────────────────────
sample_meta <- data.frame(
  sample = bio_cols,
  group  = case_when(
    bio_cols %in% plasma_cols ~ "Plasma",
    bio_cols %in% serum_cols  ~ "Serum",
    bio_cols %in% wb_cols     ~ "Whole Blood"
  ),
  label  = sub("_C18neg$", "", bio_cols),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

grp_cols <- c("Plasma" = "#0279EE", "Serum" = "#FF9400", "Whole Blood" = "#75A025")

# ── Build PCA plot for each scaling method ────────────────────────────────────
make_pca_plot <- function(mat, meta, title_label) {
  res <- run_pca(mat)
  sc  <- res$scores
  sc$sample <- rownames(sc)
  sc  <- left_join(sc, meta, by = "sample")
  ggplot(sc, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = group)) +
    geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = label), size = 2.2,
                    max.overlaps = 20, show.legend = FALSE) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = grp_cols, name = "Matrix") +
    labs(title = title_label,
         x = paste0("PC1 (", res$var_exp[1], "%)"),
         y = paste0("PC2 (", res$var_exp[2], "%)")) +
    theme_prism(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position = "right",
          plot.title      = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"))
}

# ── Boxplot: feature value distributions per sample, one panel per scaling ────
# This shows how each scaling method changes the spread and centering of
# feature values across samples. Key observations:
#   - No scaling: medians differ between matrices (intensity-driven)
#   - Mean centering: all medians shift to ~0, spread unchanged
#   - Auto-scaling: all features have unit variance; spread is equalized
#   - Pareto scaling: intermediate spread, less aggressive than auto-scaling

mat_to_long_scaling <- function(mat, stage_label) {
  as.data.frame(mat) %>%
    rownames_to_column("feature") %>%
    pivot_longer(-feature, names_to = "sample", values_to = "value") %>%
    mutate(
      stage = stage_label,
      group = case_when(
        sample %in% plasma_cols ~ "Plasma",
        sample %in% serum_cols  ~ "Serum",
        sample %in% wb_cols     ~ "Whole Blood"
      ),
      sample_short = factor(
        sub("_C18neg$", "", sample),
        levels = sub("_C18neg$", "", c(plasma_cols, serum_cols, wb_cols))
      )
    )
}

df_scaling <- bind_rows(
  mat_to_long_scaling(scale_none(mat_pqn_log2_bio),   "No additional scaling"),
  mat_to_long_scaling(scale_center(mat_pqn_log2_bio), "Mean centering"),
  mat_to_long_scaling(scale_auto(mat_pqn_log2_bio),   "Auto-scaling (UV)"),
  mat_to_long_scaling(scale_pareto(mat_pqn_log2_bio), "Pareto scaling")
) %>%
  mutate(
    stage = factor(stage, levels = c("No additional scaling", "Mean centering",
                                     "Auto-scaling (UV)", "Pareto scaling")),
    group = factor(group, levels = c("Plasma", "Serum", "Whole Blood"))
  )

p_box_scaling <- ggplot(df_scaling, aes(x = sample_short, y = value, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.2, outlier.alpha = 0.25, linewidth = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ stage, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = grp_cols, name = "Matrix") +
  labs(
    title    = "Feature Value Distributions After Centering & Scaling",
    subtitle = "Each box = one sample | PQN-normalized, log2-transformed input",
    x = "Sample", y = "Feature value"
  ) +
  theme_prism(base_size = 8) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, size = 5.5),
    strip.text      = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title      = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey40"),
    panel.spacing   = unit(0.6, "lines")
  )

# ── PCA panel (2x2) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_none   <- make_pca_plot(scale_none(mat_pqn_log2_bio),   sample_meta, "No additional scaling")
p_center <- make_pca_plot(scale_center(mat_pqn_log2_bio), sample_meta, "Mean centering")
p_auto   <- make_pca_plot(scale_auto(mat_pqn_log2_bio),   sample_meta, "Auto-scaling (UV)")
p_pareto <- make_pca_plot(scale_pareto(mat_pqn_log2_bio), sample_meta, "Pareto scaling")

p_pca_panel <- (p_none + p_center) / (p_auto + p_pareto) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Effect of Centering & Scaling on PCA",
    subtitle = "PQN-normalized, log2-transformed | Biological samples only | PCA always mean-centered",
    theme    = theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey40")
    )
  ) & theme(legend.position = "right")

# ── Combine boxplot + PCA side by side ────────────────────────────────────────
fig1 <- p_box_scaling + p_pca_panel +
  plot_layout(widths = c(1.1, 1)) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Centering & Scaling: Effect on Feature Distributions and PCA",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"))
  )

ggsave("fig1_scaling_comparison.png", fig1,
       width = 20, height = 14, dpi = 180, bg = "white")
cat("Figure 1 saved: fig1_scaling_comparison.png\n")

```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 5: Normalization Comparison Boxplot
# =============================================================================
# Boxplots of feature intensity distributions per sample are a standard QC
# visualization in metabolomics. After successful normalization, all sample
# boxes should have similar medians and spread.

# ── Build long-format data for all three stages ────────────────────────────────
mat_to_long <- function(mat, stage_label) {
  as.data.frame(mat) %>%
    rownames_to_column("feature") %>%
    pivot_longer(-feature, names_to = "sample", values_to = "intensity") %>%
    mutate(
      stage = stage_label,
      group = case_when(
        sample %in% qc_cols     ~ "QC",
        sample %in% plasma_cols ~ "Plasma",
        sample %in% serum_cols  ~ "Serum",
        sample %in% wb_cols     ~ "Whole Blood"
      ),
      sample_short = sub("_C18neg$", "", sample)
    )
}

df_raw <- mat_to_long(mat_raw_log2[, all_cols], "Raw (log2)")
df_tic <- mat_to_long(mat_tic_log2[, all_cols], "TIC (log2)")
df_pqn <- mat_to_long(mat_pqn_log2[, all_cols], "PQN (log2)")

df_all <- bind_rows(df_raw, df_tic, df_pqn) %>%
  mutate(
    stage = factor(stage, levels = c("Raw (log2)", "TIC (log2)", "PQN (log2)")),
    group = factor(group, levels = c("QC", "Plasma", "Serum", "Whole Blood")),
    sample_short = factor(sample_short,
      levels = sub("_C18neg$", "", c(qc_cols, plasma_cols, serum_cols, wb_cols)))
  )

grp_cols_all <- c("QC" = "#000000", "Plasma" = "#0279EE",
                  "Serum" = "#FF9400", "Whole Blood" = "#75A025")

fig2 <- ggplot(df_all, aes(x = sample_short, y = intensity, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.3, outlier.alpha = 0.3, linewidth = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ stage, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = grp_cols_all, name = "Matrix") +
  labs(
    title    = "Feature Intensity Distributions Before and After Normalization",
    subtitle = "Each box = one sample | log2-transformed intensities",
    x        = "Sample",
    y        = "log2(Intensity)"
  ) +
  theme_prism(base_size = 9) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x   = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, size = 6),
    strip.text    = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40"),
    panel.spacing = unit(0.8, "lines")
  )

ggsave("fig2_normalization_boxplot.png", fig2,
       width = 14, height = 11, dpi = 180, bg = "white")
cat("Figure 2 saved: fig2_normalization_boxplot.png\n")

```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 6: PCA Plots
# =============================================================================
# We use PQN-normalized, log2-transformed, Pareto-scaled data for final PCA.
#
# Plot A: All samples including QC
#   - QC samples should cluster tightly near the center of the plot.
#   - Tight QC clustering = good analytical reproducibility.
#
# Plot B: Biological samples only
#   - Reveals the metabolic differences between blood matrices.

mat_all_scaled <- scale_pareto(mat_pqn_log2[, all_cols])
mat_bio_scaled <- scale_pareto(mat_pqn_log2_bio)

# ── PCA A: All samples including QC ───────────────────────────────────────────
res_all <- run_pca(mat_all_scaled)
sc_all  <- res_all$scores
sc_all$sample <- rownames(sc_all)
sc_all$group  <- case_when(
  sc_all$sample %in% qc_cols     ~ "QC",
  sc_all$sample %in% plasma_cols ~ "Plasma",
  sc_all$sample %in% serum_cols  ~ "Serum",
  sc_all$sample %in% wb_cols     ~ "Whole Blood"
)
sc_all$label <- sub("_C18neg$", "", sc_all$sample)

p_all <- ggplot(sc_all, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = group)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = ifelse(group == "QC", 3.5, 2.5)), alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_size_identity() +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = label), size = 2.4,
                  max.overlaps = 30, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = grp_cols_all, name = "Matrix") +
  labs(
    title    = "PCA - All Samples Including QC",
    subtitle = "PQN-normalized | log2-transformed | Pareto-scaled",
    x = paste0("PC1 (", res_all$var_exp[1], "%)"),
    y = paste0("PC2 (", res_all$var_exp[2], "%)")
  ) +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        plot.title      = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40"))

# ── PCA B: Biological samples only ────────────────────────────────────────────
res_bio <- run_pca(mat_bio_scaled)
sc_bio  <- res_bio$scores
sc_bio$sample <- rownames(sc_bio)
sc_bio$group  <- case_when(
  sc_bio$sample %in% plasma_cols ~ "Plasma",
  sc_bio$sample %in% serum_cols  ~ "Serum",
  sc_bio$sample %in% wb_cols     ~ "Whole Blood"
)
sc_bio$label <- sub("_C18neg$", "", sc_bio$sample)

grp_cols_bio <- grp_cols_all[c("Plasma", "Serum", "Whole Blood")]

p_bio <- ggplot(sc_bio, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = label), size = 2.4,
                  max.overlaps = 30, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = grp_cols_bio, name = "Matrix") +
  labs(
    title    = "PCA - Biological Samples Only",
    subtitle = "PQN-normalized | log2-transformed | Pareto-scaled",
    x = paste0("PC1 (", res_bio$var_exp[1], "%)"),
    y = paste0("PC2 (", res_bio$var_exp[2], "%)")
  ) +
  theme_prism(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        plot.title      = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40"))

fig3 <- p_all + p_bio +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Principal Component Analysis of Metabolomics Data",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"))
  )

ggsave("fig3_pca_plots.png", fig3,
       width = 14, height = 6, dpi = 180, bg = "white")
cat("Figure 3 saved: fig3_pca_plots.png\n")

```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r , echo=FALSE}
# Explore Further:

# Mass Spectrometry: A Textbook 3rd ed. 2017 Edition
# Authors: Jürgen H Gross
# https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-319-54398-7

# Siuzdak, G., Activity Metabolomics and Mass Spectrometry 2025 Edition; MCC Press: San Diego, USA, 2025
# https://doi.org/10.63025/DEUD4780

# Metabolomics Practical Guide to Design and Analysis
# https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/edit/10.1201/9781315370583/metabolomics-ron-wehrens-reza-salek

# Hands-On Programming with R
# Authors: Garrett Grolemund
# https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/

# R for Data Science (2e)
# https://r4ds.hadley.nz/

# https://huaxuyu.github.io/masscubedocs/

```
